text:
He knocked at the lady's gate,
One evening when it was late.

The youngest daughter that same night,
She went to bed to this young knight.

And in the morning, when it was day,
These words unto him she did say:
'Now you have had your will,' quoth she,
'I pray, sir knight, will you marry me?'

Fair lady Isabel sits in her bower sewing,
There she heard an elf-knight blowing his horn,
The first morning in May.

She rode till she came to the sweet river side,
Three hours before it was day.

The one of them said to his mate,
'Where shall we our breakfast take?'

She buried him before the prime,
She died herself ere evening time.

But as it fell out on last Halloween,
When the fairy court was riding by,
The queen lighted down on a daisy bank,
Not far from the tree where I used to lie.

Gloomy, gloomy was the night,
And eerie was the way,
As fair Jenny in her green mantle
To Miles Cross she did go.

'Just at the mirk and midnight hour
The fairy folk will ride,
And they that wad their true-love win,
At Miles Cross they maun bide.'

'But the night is Halloween, lady,
The morn is Hallowday;
Then win me, win me, an ye will,
For weel I wat ye may.

O they begin at sky-setting,
Ride all the evening tide;
And she that will her true-love borrow,
At Miles Cross will him bide.

There was a knight and a lady bright,
Had a true tryst at the broom;
The one rode early in the morning,
The other in the afternoon.

Late late yestreen I saw the new moon,
Wi the auld moon in her arm,
And I fear, I fear, my dear master,
That we will come to harm.

Sweet Willie and Fair Annie,
As they sat on yonder hill,
If they had sat from morn till evening,
They had not talked their fill.

As it fell out on a long summer's day,
Two lovers they sat on a hill;
They sat together that long summer's day,
And could not talk their fill.

Fair Isabell of Rochroyall,
She dreamed where she lay,
She dreamed a dream of her love Gregory,
A little before the day.

The night was dark, and the wind blew cold,
And her love was fast asleep,
And the bairn that was in her two arms
Full sore began to weep.

The wind doth blow today, my love,
And a few small drops of rain;
I never had but one true-love,
In cold grave she was lain.

'Tis down in yonder garden green,
Love, where we used to walk,
The finest flower that e'er was seen
Is withered to a stalk.

The hallow day of Yule are come,
The nights are long and dark.

The young cock crew in the merry morning,
And the wild fowl chirped for day;
The elder to the younger did say,
Dear brother, we must away.

When supper was over, and mass was sung,
And every man bound for bed,
Little Musgrave and that lady
In one chamber were laid.

It was in and about the Martinmas time,
When the green leaves were a-falling.

All in the merry month of May,
When green leaves they were springing,
This young man on his death-bed lay,
For the love of Barbara Allan.

It fell about the Lammas time,
When the woods grow green and yellow.

He's mounted her on a milk-white steed,
Himself on a dapple-grey,
And made her a lady of as much land
She could ride in a whole summer day.

Then ane arose at her bed-foot,
A grumbly guest I'm sure was he.
source: The Great Silkie of Sule Skerry (Child 113)

text:
And it shall come to pass on a summer's day,
When the sun shines hot on every stone.
source: The Great Silkie of Sule Skerry (Child 113)

text:
Johnie rose up in a May morning,
Called for water to wash his hands,
And he has called for his good gray hounds,
That lay bound in iron bands.

As it befell in midsummer-time,
When birds sing sweetly on every tree.

When Flora, with her fragrant flowers,
Bedecked the earth so trim and gay,
And Neptune, with his dainty showers,
Came to present the month of May.

Lord Howard then, of courage bold,
Went to the sea with pleasant cheer,
Not curbed with winter's piercing cold,
Though it was the stormy time of the year.

With pikes, and guns, and bowmen bold,
This noble Howard is gone to the sea,
On the day before Midsummer's Eve,
And out at Thames mouth sailed they.

Last night Queen Mary had four Maries,
This night she'll have but three;
There was Mary Seaton and Mary Beaton,
And Mary Carmichael, and me.

Last night I washed Queen Mary's feet,
And bore her to her bed;
This day she's given me my reward,
This gallows-tree to tread.

They hunted high, they hunted low,
They hunted up, they hunted down,
Until the day was past the prime,
And it grew late in the afternoon.

And see not ye that bonny road,
Which winds about the fernie brae?
That is the road to fair Elfland,
Where you and I this night maun gae.

Thus rode she all one winter's night,
Till Edenborough they saw in sight.

And he is on to Clyde's water,
By the lee light of the moon.

Twas on a night, an evening bright,
When the dew began to fall,
Lady Margaret was walking up and down,
Looking o'er her castle wall.

There was a knight, in a summer's night,
Appeared in a lady's hall,
As she was walking up and down,
Looking o'er her castle wall.

O May she comes, and May she goes,
Down by yon garden green,
And there she spied a gallant squire
As squire had ever been.

Lord John's mother in her bower
Was sitting all alone;
When in the silence of the night
She heard fair Ellen's moan.

They baith lay still, and slept sound,
Until the sun began to sheen;
She drew the curtains a wee bit,
And dull and drowsy was his een.

You must come into my bower
When the evening bells do ring,
And you must come into my bower
When the evening mass doth sing.

O cocks are crowing at merry midnight,
And the wild fowls herald the day;
Give me my faith and troth again,
And let me fare me on my way.

Up then crew the red, red cock,
And up and crew the gray;
The eldest to the youngest said,
'Tis time we were away.

It fell about the Martinmas,
When nights are lang and mirk,
The carlin wife's three sons came home,
And their hats were of the birch.

Methinks I hear the thresel-cock,
Methinks I hear the jay;
Methinks I hear my lord Barnard,
And I would I were away.

The bonny clerks they died that morn,
Their loves died lang ere noon.
source: The Clerk's Twa Sons o Owsenford (Child 72)

text:
There was a knight, in a summer's night,
Was riding o'er the lee,
And there he saw a bonny birdy,
Was singing upon a tree.

And he was stout, and proud-hearted,
And thought it bitterly,
And he's gone by the wan moonlight
To meet his Marjorie.

The one was killed in the morning air,
His mother died at eve,
And ere the morning bells were rung,
The threesome were all gone.

Lady Alice was sitting in her bower-window,
Mending her midnight coif,
And there she saw as fine a corpse
As ever she saw in her life.

O lay him down gently, ye six men tall,
All on the grass so green,
And tomorrow, when the sun goes down,
Lady Alice a corpse shall be seen.

When the cock had crawn, and day did dawn,
And the sun began to peep,
Then it rose him Love Gregor,
And sorely did he weep.

And about the middle time of the night
Came twenty-four good knights in;
Sir Gyles he was the foremost man,
So well he knew that gin.

Win up, my bonny boy, he says,
As quick as ever you may;
For ye maun gang for Lillie Flower,
Before the break of day.

And weel he kent that ladye feir
Amang her maidens free,
For the flower that springs in May morning
Was not sae sweet as she.

It fell about the Lammas tide,
When the muir-men win their hay,
The doughty Douglas bound him to ride
Into England, to drive a prey.

But up then spake a little page,
Before the peep of dawn:
O waken ye, waken ye, my good lord,
For Percy's hard at hand.

This deed was done at Otterburn,
About the breaking of the day;
Earl Douglas was buried at the bracken-bush,
And Percy led captive away.

In the morning when I arose,
My bonnie palace for to see,
I came unto my lord's room-door,
But he would not speak one word to me.

O waly, waly up the bank!
And waly, waly down the brae!
And waly, waly by yon burn-side,
Where me and my love were wont to gae!

Late in the evening, drinking the wine,
Or early in the morning,
They set a combat them between,
To fight it out in the dawning.

O sleep ye, wake ye, my husband?
I wish ye wake in time!
I would not for ten thousand pounds
This night ye knew my mind.

It's now ten at night, and the stars give no light,
And the bells they ring ding, dang;
He's met with some delay that caused him to stay,
But he will be here ere lang.

Flee, flee up, my bonny grey cock,
And crow when it is day;
Your neck shall be like the bonny beaten gold,
And your wings of the silver grey.

Yestreen I lay in a well-made bed,
And my good lord beside me;
This night I'll lie in a tenant's barn,
Whatever shall betide me.

Now when our lord came home at even,
He speired for his fair lady;
The ane she cried, the tither replied,
She's awa wi the gypsy laddie.

It was late, late in the evening,
Late, late in the afternoon,
There came a knight to Brown Adam's house,
And he was clad all in brown.

O up then rose him Johnie Scot,
An hour before the day,
And he is on to Fair Ellen's bower,
To hear what she did say.

She hadna been in fair France
A twelvemonth and a day,
Till there she heard the morning drum
Beat out at break of day.
source: Rose the Red and White Lily (Child 103)

text:
She's taen him to her cellar dark,
At the hour o midnight keen;
She's stabbed him with a little penknife,
And put him in the well sae deep.
source: Sir Hugh, or, The Jew's Daughter (Child 155)

text:
She dressed herself in man's array,
And to the king's court took her way,
She rode till she came to the palace gate,
One morning when it was late.

As I went out one May morning,
To view the fields and meadows gay,
I met a maid came out of the wood,
And she had lost her way.

John early in a May morning,
Looking his shoes upon,
He took him to Nottingham,
To matins all alone.

The sixth of August the muster was,
Early in a morning clear;
Our English archers their bows did bend,
And many a black blade did appear.

He had not been sailing but a short winter's night,
And part of a short winter's day,
When he espied a lofty ship,
Come sailing all along that way.

The day it is gone, and the night's come on,
And the King's court it is begun;
All the ladies in the court are going to bed,
And it's time that I were gone.

About the dead hour of the night
She heard the bridles ring;
And Janet was as glad of that
As any earthly thing.

She looked over her castle wall,
To see what she might see;
She spied her own dear lord
Come riding over the lee,
At the dead hour of the night.

A young man riding in the night,
His journey for to take,
He rode until the morning light,
For his true love's sake.

But the night was dark, and the way was sair,
And the morn came up on them unaware.

When the trumpet sounds to horse and away,
Early in the morning,
Leave thy bed and leave thy beau,
Early in the morning.

It's up then spake the foremost man,
Said, I see her standing there;
A bonny lass in a green mantle,
Combing down her yellow hair,
Early on a May morning.

O it fell ance upon a day
To the green wood she has gane,
An Willy he has followd her,
With the clear light o the moon.

But the cocks they crew, and the horns blew,
And the lions took the hill,
And Willie he gaed hame again,
To his hard task and till…

The cocks do craw, and the day does daw,
And the wild fowl bodes on hill;
The lassie she followed her Sweet William,
And let the tears down fall.

As I walked forth one morning,
By one place that pleased me,
Wherein I heard a wandering wight,
Says, Christopher White is good company.

And it shall come to pass on a summer's day,
When the sun shines hot on every stone,
That I will take my little young son,
And teach him for to swim the foam.
source: The Great Silkie of Sule Skerry (Child 113)

text:
Johnie rose up in a May morning,
Called for water to wash his hands,
And he has called for his good gray hounds,
That lay bound in iron bands.

source: Robin Hood and Guy of Gisborne (Child 118)

text:
To see how these yeomen together fought
Two hours of a summer's day;
Yet neither Robin Hood nor Sir Guy
The other forced to fly away.
source: Robin Hood and Guy of Gisborne (Child 118)

text:
He leaned his back fast unto a thorn,
And his foot unto a stone,
And there he fought all a summer's day,
A summer's day so long.

As Robin Hood next morning stood,
Amongst the leaves so gay,
There did he espy the same young man
Come drooping along the way.
source: Robin Hood and Allen a Dale (Child 138)

text:
Wallace bold, upon a night,
Came riding over a linn;
And he is to his lover's bower,
And tirled at the pin.

My love she is a gentlewoman,
Has her living by the seam;
I know not how she is provided
This night for me and my foot-groom.

They ran over hills and dales,
And over mountains high,
Till they came on to London town,
At the dawn o' the third day.

One night as he lay on his bed,
In a dreary dream dreamed he
That Maisry stood by his bedside,
Upbraiding him for his inconstancy.

'Twas early on a May morning
Lady Isabel combed her hair;
But little knew she, ere the morn,
She would never comb it more.

It was in a pleasant time,
Upon a summer's day,
The noble Earl of Mar's daughter
Went forth to sport and play.

When day was gone, and night was come,
About the evening tide,
This lady spied a sprightly youth
Stand straight up by her side.

Then by there came two gentlemen,
At twelve o clock at night,
And they could neither see house nor hall,
Nor coal nor candle-light.
source: Get Up and Bar the Door (Child 275)

text:
Last Easter day, in the morning fair,
We was not far from land,
Where we spied a mermaid on the rock,
With comb and glass in hand.

When cocks did craw, and day did daw,
And mint in meadows sprang,
Young Ronald and his little wee boy
The way they rode along.

There was a farmer's son, kept sheep all on the hill,
And he walked out one May morning to see what he could kill.

As I went out one May morning,
One May morning betime,
I met a maid, from home had strayed
Just as the sun did shine.

As I walked out one midsummer's morning
For to view the fields and to take the air,
Down by the banks of the sweet primeroses,
There I beheld a most lovely fair.

I sowed the seeds of love,
And I sowed them in the spring;
I gathered them up in the morning so soon,
While the small birds so sweetly sing.

The cuckoo is a pretty bird, she singeth as she flies,
She bringeth us good tidings, she telleth us no lies;
She sucketh white flowers to make her voice clear,
And the more she cries 'cuckoo,' the summer draweth near.

And so early in the morning, at the dawning of the day,
They went out into the hayfield to have some sport and play.
source: The Trees They Do Grow High (Sharp 25)

text:
As I was walking one morning in May,
To hear the birds whistle and the nightingales sing,
I saw a young damsel, so sweetly sang she,
Down by the green bushes he thinks to meet me.

Oh, where are you going to, my pretty little dear,
With your red rosy cheeks and your coal black hair?
I'm going a-milking, kind sir, she answered me,
And it's dabbling in the dew makes the milkmaids fair.

As I walked out on a May morning,
On a May morning so early,
I overtook a pretty fair maid
Just as the day was a-dawning.

Fare you well, my dearest Nancy,
For now I must leave you,
All across the salt seas
I am bound for to go;
Don't let my long absence
Trouble and grieve you,
For I shall return in the spring,
As you know.

There's not a swathe goes round my waist,
Nor a comb goes in my hair,
Neither firelight nor candlelight
Can ease my heart's despair.
source: The Low Low Lands of Holland (Sharp 23)

text:
They let him stand till midsummer day,
Till he looked both pale and wan.
And little Sir John he growed a long beard
And so become a man.

We know by the moon that we are not too soon,
And we know by the sky that we are not too high.
We know by the stars that we are not too far,
And we know by the ground that we are within sound.

It's a rosebud in June, and violets in full bloom,
And the small birds singing love songs on each spray.

In the heat of the day when the sun shines so freely,
I met Master Kilby, so fine and so gay.

Well, if I were a turtle dove, had wings and could fly,
I would fly to my love's lodging and there I'd draw nigh.
And in her lily white arms all night I would lay
And watch those little winders till the dawning of day.

Come all you fair and tender ladies,
Take warning how you court young men.
They're like a bright star on a summer's morning,
First they appear and then they're gone.
source: Come All You Fair and Tender Ladies (Campbell/Sharp, No. 65)

text:
It was early, early all in a spring,
I went on board for to serve the king,
I left my dearest dearie behind,
That often told me her heart was mine.
source: Early, Early in the Spring (Campbell/Sharp, No. 72)

text:
Awake! Awake! You drowsy sleeper,
Awake! Awake! For it's almost day,
Why do you lie there in your slumber,
When your true-love is going away?

The night has passed and the day has come,
The morning sun do shine.
I will arise, said he, put on my clothes,
And then, sweet love, I'm gone.
source: Good Morning, My Pretty Little Miss (Campbell/Sharp, No. 107)

text:
I stepped out one evening to take the pleasant air,
I find Miss in the garden, a-viewing the lilies fair.
The gold rings on her fingers, as she come past by me,
She dropped them in my pocket, and for it I must die.

I walked out one May morning
To hear the small birds sing,
And I leaned my back 'gainst a cottage door
For to hear what they had to say.

I walked out one evening
All down the George's Square;
The mail coach ship had just arose,
When the post-boy met me there.
source: My Parents Treated Me Tenderly (Campbell/Sharp, No. 96)

text:
One night as I lay sleeping, so sound as I did sleep,
I heard the voice of my true love a-calling at my feet,
Saying: Rise up, William Riley, come go along with me
Into some foreign country land, and married we will be.

I dreamed of my true love last night,
All in my arms I had her,
But when I woke it was a dream;
I was forced to lay without her.

As she rose early the very next morning,
Just about the break of day,
And she saw her own dear William Taylor
A-walking with his lady gay.

As soon as the dewdrops
Grow on the green grass.
Last night she was with me,
But to-night she is gone.

It's a-raining, it's a-hailing,
The moon it gives no light,
Your horses can't travel
This dark, lonesome night.

The night before last I lay on a feather bed,
Lord Thomas he lay with me.
Last night I lay on a cold straw bed
And with the calves a-bawling all around me.

All on one summer's evening when the fever were a-dawning
I heard a fair maid make a mourn.
She was a-weeping for her father and a-grieving for her mother,
And a-thinking all on her true love John.

One cold winter night when I was a-riding
And a-drinking of good wine,
And a-thinking of the pretty little girl
That stole that heart of mine.

Last night while you were sweetly sleeping
Dreaming of some sweet repose,
While me a poor girl broken, broken hearted,
Listen to the wind that blows.

Come in, come in, my own true love,
And stay all night with me,
For I have a bed and a very fine bed,
And I'll give it up to thee.

Rise up, rise up, said the oldest one,
I think it's almost day.
See my Saviour standing by
To welcome us three home.
source: The Wife of Usher's Well (Campbell/Sharp, No. 19)

text:
On old Christmas morning my birthday shall be,
When the hills and high mountains shall bow unto me.

She rode on the milk-white steed,
And by her went the bay,
And together they came to her father's castle
Three hours before it was day.
source: Lady Isabel and the Elf Knight (Campbell/Sharp, No. 2)

text:
Crow up, crow up, my little bird,
And don't you crow before day,
And your cage shall be made of the glittering gold,
And your doors of the silver so gay.

O she took hold of his white holland sheet,
And tied it around her tight,
And it's over the hills of a cold winter's night
In a dead man's company.

He had not been sailing but a long winter's night
And part of a short winter's day
When a long, lofty ship he chanced to spy,
Came bearing down under their lee.

The winter's gone and past and the summer's come at last,
And the small birds are singing on each tree,
And the hearts of those are glad, but mine is low and sad
Since my true love is sent far from me.
source: The Winter's Gone and Past (Karpeles Newfoundland, No. 10)

text:
As I roved out one evening, all in the month of May,
The trees were in full blossom and the meadows in full gay,
I fell in love with Nancy, she's the girl whom I adore,
She is my joy and fancy. How can I love her more?

Lord Robert he died about midnight,
Lady Margaret she died before day.
I hope every couple that may now be together
May find more enjoyment than they.

How can I come downstairs
On a cold winter's night,
No spark of fire burning,
No candle alight.
You've got two white holland sheets
As white as the snow,
I pray you come down
By the light of them so.

As I roved out one morning in Spring,
To hear the larks whistle and the nightingales sing,
O green grow the rushes all covered with young,
And small birds all around me how joyful they sound.

It's early, early all in the Spring,
My love roved out for to serve the king,
With the raging seas and the winds blowing high,
Which parted me from my sailor boy.

As I walked forth one summer's morn
Hard by a river side,
Where yellow cowslips did adorn
The blushing field with pride,
I spied a damsel on the grass,
More blooming than the may;
Her looks the Queen of Love surpassed,
Among the new-mown hay.
source: The New-Mown Hay (Gardiner, Folk-Songs from Hampshire)

text:
As I was a-walking one morning in spring
To hear the small birds whistle and the nightingales sing,
I heard a fair maid making sorrowful moan,
Saying, "I am a stranger and far from my home."
source: The Happy Stranger (Gardiner, Folk-Songs from Hampshire)

text:
One morning this squire all on the highway
He met the poor thresherman and unto him did say,
"You have many children and that I well do know,
How can you maintain them as well as you do?"
source: The Squire and the Thresher (Gardiner, Folk-Songs from Hampshire)

text:
And when I come home from my labour at night
To my wife and my family in whom I take delight,
When my children come around me with their prittle-prattling noise,
Now these are the riches a poor man enjoys.
source: The Squire and the Thresher (Gardiner, Folk-Songs from Hampshire)

text:
Last night as I lay on the prairie,
And looked at the stars in the sky,
I wondered if ever a cowboy
Would drift to that sweet by and by.

As I was a-walking one morning for pleasure,
I spied a cow-puncher a-riding alone;
His hat was throwed back and his spurs was a-jingling,
As he approached me a-singing this song.
source: Whoopee-Ti-Yi-Yo, Git Along Little Dogies (Lomax, Cowboy Songs)

text:
How often at night when the heavens are bright
With the light from the glittering stars,
Have I stood here amazed and asked as I gazed
If their glory exceeds that of ours.
source: Home on the Range (Brewster Higley; Lomax, Cowboy Songs)

text:
Oh, lay still, dogies, since you have laid down,
Stretch away out on the big open ground;
Snore loud, little dogies, and drown the wild sound
That will all go away when the day rolls round.
source: Night-Herding Song (Harry Stephens; Lomax, Cowboy Songs)

text:
Yes, we buried him there on the lone prairie
Where the owl all night hoots mournfully,
And the blizzard beats and the winds blow free
O'er his lowly grave on the lone prairie.

Think I heard the noisy cook say,
"Wake up, boys, it's near the break of day" —
Way up on the Kansas line,
And slowly we will rise with the sleepy feeling eyes,
Way up on the Kansas line.

Oh, we're up in the morning ere breaking of day,
The chuck-wagon's busy, the flapjacks in play;
The herd is astir o'er hillside and vale,
With the night riders rounding them into the trail.
source: The Railroad Corral (Joseph Mills Hanson, 1904; Lomax, Cowboy Songs)
The Railroad Corral (Joseph Mills Hanson, 1904; Lomax, Cowboy Songs)

text:
Next morning, just at daybreak, we found where Rocket fell
Down in the washout twenty feet below;
Beneath the horse's body, a-lying where he fell,
Was our little Texas stray, poor wrangler Joe.
source: Little Joe the Wrangler (N. Howard Thorp, 1898; Lomax, Cowboy Songs)

text:
I'm up in the mornin' afore daylight,
And afore I sleep the moon shines bright.
source: The Old Chisholm Trail (Lomax, Cowboy Songs)
When the Work is Done This Fall (D.J. O'Malley, 1893; Lomax, Cowboy Songs)

text:
Poor Charlie was buried at sunrise, no tombstone at his head,
Nothing but a little board; and this is what it said:
"Charlie died at daybreak, he died from a fall,
And he'll not see his mother when the work's all done this fall."
source: When the Work is Done This Fall (D.J. O'Malley, 1893; Lomax, Cowboy Songs)

text:
'Twas on one Sunday morning, as you shall quickly hear,
Our logs were piled up mountain-high, we could not keep them clear;
"Turn out, brave boys," the foreman cried, with a voice devoid of fear,
"And we'll break up the jam on Gerry's rock and for Eagletown we'll steer."

As I walked out over London Bridge,
One misty morning early,
I overheard a fair, pretty maid
Was lamenting for her Geordie.

Early one morning before the break of day,
He went to his Polly, these words he did say:
"O Polly, O Polly, you must go with me,
Before we are married my friends for to see."

Little Matty Groves he sleeps tonight,
Keeping your lady warm.
source: Little Musgrave and Lady Barnard (Sharp/Karpeles, No. 23)

text:
Sweet William rose one morning bright
And dressed himself in blue.
source: Fair Margaret and Sweet William (Sharp/Karpeles, No. 17)

text:
The day being gone and the night being come,
When most men were asleep,
Sweet William spied Lady Margaret's ghost
A-standing at his bed feet.
source: Fair Margaret and Sweet William (Sharp/Karpeles, No. 17)

text:
Every night when the sun goes in,
Every night when the sun goes in,
I hang down my head and mournful cry.
source: Every Night When the Sun Goes In (Sharp/Karpeles)

text:
The chickens they are crowing, a-crowing, a-crowing,
The chickens they are crowing, for it is almost daylight.

Bright morning stars are rising,
Bright morning stars are rising,
Bright morning stars are rising,
Day is a-breaking in my soul.

One morning in May, as I carelessly did stray,
I met a bold grenadier on my way.

Upon a Sunday morning, when Spring was in its prime,
Along the Church-lane tripping, I heard the Church-bells chime.
source: Upon a Sunday Morning (Songs of the West, No. 3)

text:
And we were up as soon as the day,
For to fetch the Summer home, O!
The Summer, and the May,
Now the Winter is a gone, O!
source: The Hal-an-Tow (Songs of the West, No. 24)

text:
On a moon-shiny night, O! 'tis my delight,
A-catching o' moles.
source: The Mole-Catcher (Songs of the West, No. 45)

text:
The fox went out one winter night,
And prayed the moon to give him light,
For he'd many a mile to go that night,
Before he reached his den, O!
source: The Fox (Songs of the West, No. 55)

text:
Come all you young fellows that carry a gun,
Beware of late shooting when daylight is done;
For 'tis little you reckon what hazards you run,
I shot my true love at the setting of the sun.
source: At the Setting of the Sun (Songs of the West, No. 62)

text:
As I walked out one May morning,
One May morning so early;
I there espied a fair pretty maid,
All on the dew so pearly.
O! 'twas sweet, sweet spring,
Merry birds did sing,
All in the morning early.
source: On a May Morning So Early (Songs of the West, No. 73)

text:
'Twas early I walked on a midsummer morning,
The fields and the meadows were deckèd and gay,
The small birds were singing, the woodlands a-ringing,
'Twas early in the morning, at breaking of day.
source: Midsummer Carol (Songs of the West, No. 89)

text:
Tobacco is an Indian weed,
Grows green at morn, is cut down at eve.
source: Tobacco is an Indian Weed (Songs of the West, No. 95)

text:
The golden sun is shining bright,
The dew is off the field;
To us it is our main delight,
The fork and rake to wield.
The pipe and tabor both shall play,
The viols loudly ring,
From morn till eve each summer day,
As we go hay-making.
source: The Merry Haymakers (Songs of the West, No. 109)

text:
It is cold winter's night,
You and I must bide alone:
Whilst my pretty lad is young
And is growing.
source: The Trees They Are So High (Songs of the West, No. 4)

text:
Beside the rolling ocean
One morning in the month of June,
The feathered warbling songsters
Were sweetly changing note and tune.
source: The Bonny Bunch of Roses (Songs of the West, No. 27)

text:
As I was a walking one morning in May,
To hear the birds whistle, see lambkins at play,
I spied a fair damsel, O sweetly sang she—
'Down by the green bushes he thinks to meet me.'
source: The Green Bushes (Songs of the West, No. 43)

text:
Awake, awake ye pretty maids,
And take the May-bush in,
Or 'twill be gone ere tomorrow morn,
And you'll have none within.
source: May-Day Carol (Songs of the West, No. 47)

text:
Come awaken in the morn,
When the cock upon the dunghill
Is a-blowing of his horn.
source: The Oxen Ploughing (Songs of the West, No. 57)

text:
'Twas early one morning at breaking of day,
The cocks were a crowing, the farmer did say,
Come, arise, my good fellows, arise with good will,
For your horses want something their bellies to fill.
source: Jolly Fellows That Follow the Plough (Songs of the West, No. 63)

text:
One morning so gay, in the glad month of May,
When I from my cottage strayed;
As broke the ray of awakening day,
I met a pretty maid.
source: The Spotted Cow (Songs of the West, No. 74)

text:
All in a morning very fair
As I rode out to take the air
I heard some to halloo clearly.
source: The Duke's Hunt (Songs of the West, No. 81)

text:
As I walked out one morn betime,
To view the fields in May, Sir,
There I espied a fair sweet maid,
Among the new-mown hay, Sir.
source: Among the New-Mown Hay (Songs of the West, No. 102)

text:
Within a garden a maiden lingered,
When soft the shades of evening fell,
Expecting, fearing,
A footstep hearing,
Her love appearing,
To say farewell.
source: Within a Garden (Songs of the West, No. 105)

text:
For all the day long she sits in a tree,
And when the night cometh, away flies she.
source: The Owl (Songs of the West, No. 115)

text:
The lark in the morn ascendeth on high
And leaves the poor owl to sob and to sigh;
And all the day long, the owl is asleep,
While little birds blithely are singing, cheep! cheep!
source: The Owl (Songs of the West, No. 115)

text:
By chance it was I met my love,
It did me much surprise,
Down by a shady myrtle grove,
Just as the sun did rise.
source: By Chance It Was (Songs of the West, No. 1)

text:
When the wind whistles cold on the moor of a night,
Tom Pearce's old mare doth appear, gashly white.
source: Widdecombe Fair (Songs of the West, No. 16)

text:
Come to my window, my Love, O my Love,
Come to my window, my Dear.
For my mammy is asleep,
And my daddy snoreth deep,
Then come, e'er the day-light appear.
source: Come to My Window (Songs of the West, No. 41)

text:
Moon shining bright,
Dropping of dew.
Owls hoot all night
To-whit! to-whoo!

I sit by the cradle, my baby's asleep,
And rocking the cradle, I wonder and weep.
O little blue light,
In the dead of the night,
O prithee, O prithee no nearer to creep.
source: The Blue Flame (Songs of the West, No. 67)

text:
Why follow the church path, why steal you this way?
Why halt in your journey, on threshold why stay?
With flicker and flare,
Why dance up my stair!
O I would, O I would, it were dawning of day.
source: The Blue Flame (Songs of the West, No. 67)

text:
At break of day each summer's morning
William for his horses went,
And as he viewed bright Phoebus dawning,
He would listen with content
To the voice of sweet Rosetta,
Which charmed young William's heart with joy;
With voice so shrill she loved young Will,
Who was her father's gay ploughboy.
source: Rosetta and Her Gay Ploughboy (Broadwood, English Traditional Songs and Carols)

text:
She sat and sung of her sweet William,
As she milked her spotted cow;
And he would sigh for his Rosetta
All the day while at the plough;
And as evening did approach,
Rosetta tript along with joy,
With voice so shrill, to meet young Will,
Who was her father's gay ploughboy.
source: Rosetta and Her Gay Ploughboy (Broadwood, English Traditional Songs and Carols)

text:
As I rode over Banstead Downs,
One mid-May morning early,
There I espied a pretty fair maid
Lamenting for her Georgie.
source: Georgie (Broadwood, English Traditional Songs and Carols)

text:
Oh the moon shines bright, and the stars give a light;
Oh, a little before the day,
Our Lord, our God, He calls upon us all,
And He bids us awake and pray.
source: The Moon Shines Bright (Broadwood, English Traditional Songs and Carols)

text:
I've been rambling all the night,
And the best part of the day;
And now I am returning back again,
I have brought you a branch of May.
source: Bedfordshire May Day Carol (Broadwood, English Traditional Songs and Carols)
